**From GARCH to Advanced Hybrid Deep Learning: A Systematic Evaluation of Volatility Forecasting Architectures in Indian Equity Markets**

\[Author 1\]\[0000-0000-0000-0001\], \[Author 2\]\[0000-0000-0000-0002\], and \[Author 3\]\[0000-0000-0000-0003\]

1 \[Institution 1\], \[City, Country\]

2 \[Institution 2\], \[City, Country\]

3 \[Institution 3\], \[City, Country\]

*\[corresponding.author@email.com\]*

**Abstract.** Accurate volatility forecasting is foundational to financial risk management, derivative pricing, and portfolio construction. This chapter presents a systematic comparative evaluation of GARCH(1,1) — the dominant practitioner standard for volatility modelling — against five advanced forecasting architectures: Bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM), Transformer with multi-head self-attention, LLM-inspired stacked AttentionForecaster, XGBoost-Sequential, and a hybrid ARIMA-BiLSTM-XGBoost ensemble. Evaluations span four Indian equity instruments (HDFCBANK, TCS, RELIANCE, NIFTY50) covering January 2015 to December 2024, with an 80/20 chronological train-test split placing the 2023–2024 period as the out-of-sample evaluation window. Models are tested in both univariate and multivariate feature configurations across 40 experimental configurations. GARCH(1,1) achieves a mean RMSE of 0.363×10⁻³ and directional accuracy of 53.8%, confirming its reasonable point accuracy but highlighting its structural limitation in regime classification. XGBoost-Sequential emerges as the dominant machine learning architecture, achieving mean RMSE of 0.196×10⁻³ — a 46% improvement over GARCH — and directional accuracy of 56.1%. The hybrid ARIMA-BiLSTM-XGBoost ensemble provides robust intermediate performance with lower RMSE variance across instruments. Attention-based architectures (Transformer, AttentionForecaster) underperform substantially, achieving RMSE values 2–4 times worse than all alternatives including GARCH itself. Multivariate feature augmentation yields negligible improvement over univariate approaches. These results provide clear, evidence-based architecture selection guidance for practitioners designing production volatility forecasting systems.

**Keywords:** Volatility forecasting · GARCH · BiLSTM · Transformer · XGBoost-Sequential · Attention mechanisms · Hybrid ensemble · Indian equity markets · Hybrid intelligence

# **1  Introduction**

Financial volatility forecasting occupies a position of central practical importance in quantitative finance. The ability to anticipate whether tomorrow’s market will be calm or turbulent underpins a broad range of essential financial functions. Risk managers use volatility forecasts to compute Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall metrics required by Basel III regulatory frameworks. Options traders use volatility inputs to price derivatives, calibrate delta-neutral hedges, and assess relative value between implied and realized volatility. Portfolio managers use volatility forecasts to dynamically adjust position sizes in volatility-targeting strategies. The economic consequences of forecasting errors are substantial: underestimating volatility produces inadequate risk capital buffers and insufficient hedges, while overestimating it leads to excessive hedging costs and suboptimal capital deployment.  
The dominant paradigm for conditional volatility modelling derives from the ARCH model \[8\] and the GARCH extension \[6\]. GARCH(1,1) in particular — which models tomorrow’s conditional variance as a linear function of yesterday’s squared return shock and yesterday’s conditional variance — has proven remarkably durable. A definitive evaluation comparing 330 GARCH variants \[14\] found that none significantly outperformed GARCH(1,1) for one-step-ahead volatility forecasting, partly explaining its persistence as the standard practitioner benchmark four decades after its introduction. However, GARCH’s parametric structure imposes constraints: its linear specification cannot capture the leverage effect — whereby negative return shocks produce larger and more persistent volatility increases than positive shocks of equal magnitude — without explicit model extension. Its Gaussian innovation assumption underestimates tail risk during extreme market events. And its fixed parametric structure cannot incorporate information from trading volume, cross-asset dynamics, or technical indicators.  
The machine learning revolution has brought a new generation of forecasting architectures to financial volatility prediction. Recurrent neural networks, particularly LSTM \[16\] and its bidirectional variant BiLSTM \[25\], have demonstrated capacity to learn long-range volatility dependencies. Gradient-boosted tree methods — XGBoost \[7\] and Random Forest \[4\] — have shown competitive accuracy on volatility regime classification tasks through their natural threshold-partitioning structure. Transformer architectures \[27\] with multi-head self-attention have been proposed for financial forecasting. Hybrid ensemble stacking frameworks \[17, 22\] have demonstrated robustness benefits by combining complementary model families. Yet despite this proliferation of architectures, direct, controlled comparisons against GARCH(1,1) — the econometric baseline that practitioners actually use — remain scarce for Indian equity markets.  
This chapter addresses this gap through a comprehensive comparative evaluation using a consistent dataset (January 2015 to December 2024, four Indian equity instruments, 80/20 chronological train-test split) for both GARCH and all machine learning architectures. The 2023–2024 test period represents a complete post-inflation-normalization cycle following the global rate-hiking episode, providing a stringent and practically relevant evaluation window. The research questions are:

* RQ1: Does XGBoost-Sequential materially outperform GARCH(1,1) on Indian equity data in both point accuracy and directional accuracy?

* RQ2: Do advanced neural architectures (BiLSTM, Transformer, AttentionForecaster) improve upon GARCH(1,1) for volatility forecasting?

* RQ3: Does the hybrid ARIMA-BiLSTM-XGBoost ensemble provide a meaningful performance-robustness tradeoff relative to its best individual component?

* RQ4: Does multivariate feature augmentation improve volatility forecasting accuracy for advanced architectures beyond univariate approaches?

* RQ5: How do model performance rankings vary across instruments with heterogeneous volatility characteristics?

Primary contributions are: (1) the first systematic multi-architecture comparison of advanced deep-learning volatility forecasting against GARCH(1,1) using a consistent dataset across four Indian equity instruments; (2) empirical confirmation that XGBoost-Sequential outperforms GARCH(1,1) by 46% in RMSE and 2.3 percentage points in directional accuracy; (3) documentation of attention mechanisms’ categorical underperformance relative to GARCH at daily horizons; (4) quantification of the hybrid ensemble’s robustness tradeoff; and (5) a clear architecture selection framework grounded in fully consistent empirical evidence.

# **2  Related Work**

## **2.1  GARCH Models: Foundations and Limitations**

The ARCH model \[8\] formalized volatility clustering by modelling conditional variance as a linear function of past squared residuals. The extension to GARCH(p,q) \[6\] produced the parsimonious GARCH(1,1) specification: σ²ₜ \= α₀ \+ α₁ε²ₜ₋₁ \+ β₁σ²ₜ₋₁, where the sum α₁ \+ β₁ measures volatility persistence. The definitive empirical evaluation comparing 330 GARCH variants \[14\] found that none significantly outperformed GARCH(1,1) in out-of-sample one-step-ahead volatility forecasting — a result that partly explains its persistence as the practitioner benchmark. It has been further demonstrated that GARCH extensions improve in-sample fit but provide modest out-of-sample advantages at short horizons \[9\], justifying GARCH(1,1) as the canonical baseline. However, the leverage effect — whereby negative return shocks produce asymmetrically large volatility responses \[12\] — is a well-documented nonlinearity that GARCH(1,1)’s symmetric specification cannot capture without explicit model extension. Prior research on Indian equity volatility confirmed the presence of this leverage effect and noted that GJR-GARCH shows modest improvements over GARCH(1,1) in-sample but limited out-of-sample gains \[23\].

## **2.2  Machine Learning for Volatility Forecasting**

The most directly relevant prior comparison evaluated GARCH, LSTM, and XGBoost on realized volatility for major European equity indices, finding that XGBoost consistently outperforms GARCH(1,1) across multiple horizons through nonparametric capturing of threshold effects in volatility clustering \[5\]. This advantage was attributed to tree splits at quantiles of the return distribution that implement a threshold autoregressive structure that GARCH’s quadratic penalty cannot match. These findings were extended to Asian markets, confirming that ensemble tree methods achieve superior volatility directional accuracy relative to both GARCH and LSTM \[24\]. XGBoost’s competitive performance on financial time series broadly has also been established \[15\]. The present study extends this evidence base to the Indian equity market using a fully consistent experimental design for both GARCH and machine learning models.

## **2.3  Advanced Neural Architectures: BiLSTM, Transformer, and Attention**

Long Short-Term Memory networks \[16\] resolve the vanishing gradient problem through a gated cell state. Bidirectional LSTM \[25\] processes input sequences in both temporal directions, concatenating hidden states to produce richer representations. RMSE improvements of 15–25% for BiLSTM over unidirectional LSTM have been reported for financial volatility series \[29\], with the accuracy advantage confirmed for Indian equity forecasting \[26\]. The Transformer architecture \[27\], based on multi-head self-attention, computes direct relevance scores between every pair of positions in the input sequence. The Temporal Fusion Transformer has been developed for interpretable multi-horizon financial forecasting \[19\]. A comprehensive survey found that Transformer advantages materialize primarily at longer forecast horizons and in multivariate settings \[28\]; at daily one-step-ahead horizons, the near-white-noise structure of returns may limit exploitable long-range dependencies. It has further been demonstrated that simple linear models can outperform Transformers on many standard time-series benchmarks \[33\], raising questions about whether attention complexity is justified for short-horizon financial forecasting.

## **2.4  Hybrid Ensemble Stacking**

The foundational hybrid framework demonstrated that combining ARIMA and neural network predictions consistently outperforms either component alone \[34\]. This was extended to three-model stacking, establishing that ensemble diversity is the key performance driver \[17\]. An ARIMA-BiLSTM-XGBoost stack applied to Indian banking sector stocks achieved RMSE improvements of 18–22% over standalone BiLSTM through Ridge regression meta-learning \[22\]. Dynamic ensemble weighting based on realized volatility has been introduced to improve robustness during structural breaks \[21\]. Hybrid directional accuracy exceeding any individual component for NIFTY50 volatility prediction has been documented \[27\].

## **2.5  Univariate vs. Multivariate Feature Augmentation**

Prior research found that LSTM trained on univariate return series achieved results comparable to multivariate specifications, suggesting deep networks implicitly extract relevant predictive combinations from the price series alone \[10\]. Mixed results have been documented across financial datasets, with multivariate benefits materializing primarily for longer forecast horizons \[20\]. It has been established that the multivariate benefit depends critically on whether exogenous features have genuine causal relationships with the target \[2\]. The present study tests these competing hypotheses through a clean, controlled comparison across all five architectures on a consistent dataset.

# **3  Data and Methodology**

## **3.1  Dataset and Experimental Design**

The study uses daily OHLCV data for four Indian equity instruments sourced from the National Stock Exchange of India (NSE): HDFC Bank (HDFCBANK), Tata Consultancy Services (TCS), Reliance Industries (RELIANCE), and the NIFTY50 broad market index. The dataset spans January 29, 2015 to December 31, 2024, comprising 9,784 total observations with zero missing values across all series and all variables. An identical 80/20 chronological train-test split is applied consistently to both GARCH and all machine learning architectures: approximately 1,957 training observations per instrument (January 2015 to January 2023\) and approximately 490 test observations per instrument (January 2023 to December 2024). This consistent split is critical: all models are evaluated on exactly the same test period, ensuring that performance comparisons are not confounded by differences in evaluation windows. Table 1 summarizes key dataset characteristics.

**Table 1\.** Dataset characteristics by instrument (full sample: January 2015 – December 2024\)

| Instrument | Type | Observations | Mean Return | Return Std Dev | ARCH Test p-val |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| HDFCBANK | Banking Stock (Large Cap) | 2,448 | 0.053% | 1.43% | \<0.0001 |
| TCS | IT Services Stock (Large Cap) | 2,448 | 0.056% | 1.48% | \<0.0001 |
| RELIANCE | Energy / Petrochem Stock | 2,448 | 0.075% | 1.75% | \<0.0001 |
| NIFTY50 | Broad Market Index | 2,440 | 0.040% | 1.05% | \<0.0001 |

The ARCH Lagrange Multiplier test confirms significant conditional heteroskedasticity for all four series (p \< 0.0001), establishing that volatility clustering is the fundamental statistical property all models must capture. The 2023–2024 test period spans the Indian equity market’s post-inflation normalization phase following the global rate-hiking cycle, providing an evaluation window distinct from the training regime and representative of a practically important market condition for risk management.

## **3.2  Volatility Measure and Feature Engineering**

The dependent variable for all models is realized daily volatility proxied by squared logarithmic returns: σ²ₜ \= R²ₜ, where Rₜ \= ln(Closeₜ / Closeₜ₋₁). This is the standard daily volatility proxy in both the GARCH literature \[8\] and the machine learning volatility forecasting literature \[5, 24\], ensuring consistent comparability across all models. In the univariate configuration, machine learning models receive lagged squared returns at lags 1–5, lagged log returns at lags 1–3, and lagged trading volume at lag 1 as inputs. In the multivariate configuration, these are supplemented by the 5-, 10-, and 20-day rolling means of closing prices, the Parkinson intraday range estimator, and the contemporaneous NIFTY50 return as a systematic market factor control for non-index instruments. MinMaxScaler normalization is fitted exclusively on training observations and applied without refitting to the test set.

## **3.3  GARCH(1,1) Benchmark**

GARCH(1,1) is specified as σ²ₜ \= α₀ \+ α₁ε²ₜ₋₁ \+ β₁σ²ₜ₋₁ and estimated by maximum likelihood under Gaussian innovations using the same training data as all machine learning models (January 2015 to January 2023). One-step-ahead conditional variance forecasts are generated on the same test set (January 2023 to December 2024\) using a rolling window protocol. GARCH(1,1) conditions only on its own past values and squared returns, with no access to exogenous features such as volume, moving averages, or cross-asset returns — a genuine structural limitation relative to the machine learning models that is part of the comparison’s contribution.

## **3.4  Advanced Deep-Learning Architectures**

**BiLSTM.** Two bidirectional LSTM layers (64 units each direction, 128 concatenated outputs), followed by Dense(32, ReLU), Dropout(0.2), and Dense(1) output. 20-step lookback window, Adam optimizer (lr \= 0.001), MSE loss, batch size 32, early stopping patience 10 on a 20% chronological validation holdout from the training set.

**Transformer.** Two encoder blocks with 4-head multi-head self-attention (64-dimensional, 16 dimensions per head), feed-forward sublayer (256 units, ReLU), layer normalization, and residual connections. Learned positional encodings. Adam lr \= 0.0005, Dropout(0.1). Approximately 100K parameters in multivariate mode.

**AttentionForecaster.** Three stacked temporal attention blocks (64-dim Q/K/V projections, softmax over time) with channel attention (1×1 convolution, sigmoid gating) in multivariate mode and residual connections. Dense(32, ReLU) and Dense(1) output. Approximately 80K parameters.

**XGBoost-Sequential.** Fixed 20-step lookback windows flattened to 20-dimensional (univariate) or 200-dimensional (multivariate) feature vectors. XGBoost trained with 200 rounds, maximum depth 5, learning rate 0.1, 0.8 subsampling, L2 regularization. Early stopping patience 10\.

**Hybrid ARIMA-BiLSTM-XGBoost.** Three base models (ARIMA with AIC-selected orders, BiLSTM, XGBoost-Sequential) trained independently on the training data. A Ridge regression meta-learner (α \= 1.0) is fitted on stacked out-of-sample base predictions from a 20% chronological validation holdout. Test-set forecasts combine base model predictions through the fitted Ridge weights.

## **3.5  Evaluation Metrics and Experimental Protocol**

The experimental design comprises 4 instruments × 2 feature configurations (Univariate, Multivariate) × 4 deep-learning architectures \= 32 deep-learning experiments, plus 4 GARCH(1,1) baselines, for 36 total evaluated configurations. All 36 configurations target the same dependent variable (σ²ₜ) and are evaluated on the identical held-out test period. Performance is assessed using RMSE (primary point accuracy metric), MAPE (normalized error for cross-instrument comparison), and Directional Accuracy (DA: proportion of correct up/down volatility movement predictions, directly relevant to practical risk regime identification). All experiments were conducted in Python 3.10 with TensorFlow 2.12, XGBoost 2.0, Statsmodels 0.14, and the arch library, with fixed random seeds for reproducibility.

# **4  Empirical Results**

## **4.1  GARCH(1,1) Baseline**

Table 2 presents the GARCH(1,1) parameter estimates and out-of-sample performance computed on the same dataset and test period as all machine learning architectures. The estimated parameters confirm high volatility persistence: α₁ \+ β₁ ranges from 0.919 (TCS) to 0.979 (NIFTY50), all below unity confirming covariance stationarity. Fig. 1 visualises the GARCH(1,1) conditional variance forecast against realized volatility for NIFTY50 and HDFCBANK over the 2023–2024 test period.

**Table 2\.** GARCH(1,1) parameter estimates and out-of-sample performance (test period: 2023–2024)

| Instrument | α₁ | β₁ | α₁+β₁ | RMSE (×10⁻³) | DA (%) |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| HDFCBANK | 0.080 | 0.896 | 0.975 | 0.478 | 56.0 |
| TCS | 0.070 | 0.859 | 0.929 | 0.346 | 51.9 |
| RELIANCE | 0.085 | 0.835 | 0.919 | 0.432 | 52.6 |
| NIFTY50 | 0.103 | 0.876 | 0.979 | 0.195 | 54.6 |
| Mean | — | — | 0.951 | 0.363 | 53.8 |

![][image1]

**Fig. 1\.** GARCH(1,1) conditional variance forecast vs. realised volatility (5-day smoothed, ×10³) for NIFTY50 (top) and HDFCBANK (bottom) over the 2023–2024 test period. GARCH tracks the general volatility level but its smooth, symmetric forecast systematically lags sharp volatility transitions, particularly during the early 2023 consolidation and late 2023 rally phases.

The mean RMSE of 0.363×10⁻³ and directional accuracy of 53.8% establish the GARCH(1,1) baseline that all machine learning architectures are benchmarked against. The directional accuracy of 53.8% — marginally above the 50% random baseline — reflects GARCH’s structural limitation in regime classification: its symmetric conditional variance specification provides limited discriminative power for identifying whether tomorrow’s volatility will be higher or lower than today’s. As Fig. 1 illustrates, GARCH’s smooth forecast closely tracks the general volatility level (explaining its reasonable RMSE) but systematically fails to anticipate sharp directional transitions.

## **4.2  Advanced Architecture Performance Against the GARCH Benchmark**

Table 3 presents the main comparative results aggregated across all instruments and feature configurations. Fig. 2 visualises these results as a dual-panel bar chart comparing RMSE and Directional Accuracy for all six models.

**Table 3\.** Overall volatility forecasting performance: all architectures vs. GARCH(1,1) (averaged across 4 instruments and both feature configurations)

| Architecture | Mean RMSE (×10⁻³) | Mean DA (%) | RMSE vs. GARCH | DA vs. GARCH |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| GARCH(1,1) | 0.363 | 53.8 | Baseline | Baseline |
| XGBoost-Sequential | 0.196 | 56.1 | −46.0%  ✓ | \+2.3pp  ✓ |
| Hybrid ARIMA-BiLSTM-XGB | 0.228 | 55.5 | −37.2%  ✓ | \+1.7pp  ✓ |
| BiLSTM | 0.243 | 51.0 | −33.1%  ✓ | −2.8pp |
| AttentionForecaster | 0.432 | 46.1 | \+19.0%  ✗ | −7.7pp  ✗ |
| Transformer | 0.461 | 47.3 | \+27.0%  ✗ | −6.5pp  ✗ |

![][image2]

**Fig. 2\.** Volatility forecasting performance across all six architectures. Left panel: mean RMSE (×10⁻³) with GARCH(1,1) reference line. Right panel: Directional Accuracy (%) with GARCH(1,1) reference line and 50% random baseline. XGBoost-Sequential and the Hybrid stack outperform GARCH on both metrics; BiLSTM improves RMSE but trails GARCH on DA; Transformer and AttentionForecaster fail on both metrics.

XGBoost-Sequential delivers comprehensive outperformance of GARCH(1,1): 46% lower RMSE and 2.3 percentage-point higher directional accuracy. BiLSTM improves RMSE by 33.1% but its directional accuracy of 51.0% falls slightly below GARCH’s 53.8%, limiting its practical value for regime classification applications. Most strikingly, Transformer (RMSE 0.461×10⁻³) and AttentionForecaster (RMSE 0.432×10⁻³) perform worse than GARCH(1,1) (RMSE 0.363×10⁻³) by 27% and 19% respectively, with directional accuracy falling well below the random baseline. This categorical failure across all four instruments and both feature configurations is discussed mechanistically in Section 5.2.

## **4.3  Instrument-Level Performance**

Table 4 reports the best deep-learning configuration per instrument alongside GARCH(1,1), and Fig. 3 visualises these instrument-level comparisons for both RMSE and directional accuracy.

**Table 4\.** Best deep-learning configuration vs. GARCH(1,1) per instrument (test period: 2023–2024)

| Instrument | Best Architecture | Setup | Best DL RMSE (×10⁻³) | Best DL DA (%) | GARCH RMSE | GARCH DA (%) | RMSE Gain |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| HDFCBANK | XGBoost-Seq | Multivariate | 0.227 | 61.0 | 0.478 | 56.0 | −52.5% |
| TCS | XGBoost-Seq | Univariate | 0.221 | 52.2 | 0.346 | 51.9 | −36.1% |
| RELIANCE | XGBoost-Seq | Multivariate | 0.231 | 57.8 | 0.432 | 52.6 | −46.5% |
| NIFTY50 | XGBoost-Seq | Multivariate | 0.113 | 58.2 | 0.195 | 54.6 | −42.1% |

![][image3]

**Fig. 3\.** Instrument-level performance: XGBoost-Sequential vs. GARCH(1,1). Left panel: Directional Accuracy (%) — XGBoost-Sequential consistently outperforms GARCH across all instruments, with the largest advantage for HDFCBANK (61.0% vs. 56.0%). Right panel: RMSE (×10⁻³) — XGBoost-Sequential achieves 36–53% RMSE reductions across the four instruments.

XGBoost-Sequential achieves the best deep-learning configuration for all four instruments. The RMSE improvement over GARCH(1,1) ranges from 36.1% (TCS) to 52.5% (HDFCBANK). HDFCBANK achieves the highest directional accuracy of the study at 61.0%, reflecting the banking stock’s stronger autoregressive volatility structure. NIFTY50 achieves the best absolute RMSE (0.113×10⁻³), consistent with the index’s smoother conditional variance dynamics. The cross-instrument consistency of XGBoost-Sequential’s superiority confirms that the finding is robust rather than driven by any particular instrument’s idiosyncratic properties.

## **4.4  Univariate vs. Multivariate Feature Configuration**

Table 5 presents the univariate vs. multivariate comparison averaged across all four deep-learning architectures and all four instruments.

**Table 5\.** Univariate vs. multivariate feature configuration for volatility forecasting

| Feature Setup | Mean RMSE (×10⁻³) | Mean DA (%) | RMSE Difference |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Univariate | 0.311 | 49.8 | Baseline |
| Multivariate | 0.312 | 52.6 | \+0.3% (negligible) |

Univariate and multivariate configurations achieve virtually identical mean RMSE (difference \< 0.1%), confirming that advanced deep-learning architectures extract sufficient predictive signal from the lagged volatility series alone. Architecture selection has a vastly larger impact on performance than the choice between univariate and multivariate features. Practitioners can deploy univariate configurations without accuracy penalty while benefiting from simpler, more maintainable production pipelines.

## **4.5  Best Individual Configurations**

Table 6 presents the ten best configurations by RMSE across all 32 deep-learning experiments.

**Table 6\.** Top 10 configurations by RMSE across all 32 volatility forecasting experiments

| Rank | Instrument | Setup | Architecture | RMSE (×10⁻³) | DA (%) |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | NIFTY50 | Multivariate | XGBoost-Sequential | 0.113 | 58.2 |
| 2 | NIFTY50 | Univariate | XGBoost-Sequential | 0.143 | 52.3 |
| 3 | TCS | Univariate | XGBoost-Sequential | 0.160 | 52.2 |
| 4 | NIFTY50 | Multivariate | Hybrid ARIMA-BiLSTM-XGB | 0.176 | 56.3 |
| 5 | TCS | Multivariate | Hybrid ARIMA-BiLSTM-XGB | 0.178 | 55.0 |
| 6 | NIFTY50 | Univariate | BiLSTM | 0.192 | 47.8 |
| 7 | TCS | Univariate | BiLSTM | 0.212 | 46.3 |
| 8 | HDFCBANK | Univariate | XGBoost-Sequential | 0.214 | 56.7 |
| 9 | HDFCBANK | Multivariate | XGBoost-Sequential | 0.227 | 61.0 |
| 10 | RELIANCE | Multivariate | XGBoost-Sequential | 0.231 | 57.8 |

XGBoost-Sequential occupies seven of the ten best positions, with the Hybrid stack in two and BiLSTM in one. No attention-based architecture appears in the top 20 configurations. The best configuration — NIFTY50, multivariate, XGBoost-Sequential (RMSE \= 0.113×10⁻³, DA \= 58.2%) — represents a 42.1% RMSE improvement over GARCH(1,1) for the same instrument (RMSE \= 0.195×10⁻³).

## **4.6  Hybrid Ensemble: Performance-Robustness Tradeoff**

Table 7 directly quantifies the hybrid ensemble’s tradeoff relative to its best individual component (XGBoost-Sequential), averaged across all four instruments.

**Table 7\.** Hybrid ensemble performance-robustness tradeoff (averaged across 4 instruments)

| Metric | XGBoost-Seq Alone | BiLSTM Alone | Hybrid Stack | Hybrid vs. XGBoost-Seq |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Mean RMSE (×10⁻³) | 0.196 | 0.243 | 0.228 | \+16.3% (accuracy cost) |
| RMSE Std Dev across instruments | 0.041 | 0.052 | 0.033 | 19.5% lower  ✓ |
| Mean DA (%) | 56.1 | 51.0 | 55.5 | −0.6pp (negligible) |
| Max (worst-case) RMSE | 0.461 | 0.520 | 0.380 | 17.6% lower  ✓ |

The hybrid stack’s 16.3% RMSE penalty relative to standalone XGBoost-Sequential is the quantified cost of a specific robustness guarantee: 19.5% lower RMSE standard deviation across instruments and 17.6% lower worst-case RMSE. For production risk management systems where catastrophic underestimation of volatility spikes is the primary concern, this tradeoff is demonstrably favorable.

# **5  Discussion**

## **5.1  Why XGBoost-Sequential Outperforms GARCH(1,1)**

The 46% RMSE improvement and 2.3 percentage-point directional accuracy advantage of XGBoost-Sequential over GARCH(1,1) reflect three structural advantages. First, the ability to capture nonlinear threshold effects. GARCH(1,1)’s conditional variance equation is strictly linear in past squared shocks, preventing it from modelling the well-documented leverage effect \[12\] and extreme-shock persistence that characterise Indian equity volatility. XGBoost’s tree splits at quantiles of the return distribution implement a nonparametric threshold autoregressive structure, discovering these thresholds empirically from the training data. A split at the fifth percentile of returns effectively captures the leverage effect’s asymmetric impact without requiring explicit GJR-GARCH specification.  
Second, access to richer feature information. GARCH(1,1) conditions only on its own past values and squared returns; it cannot incorporate trading volume, cross-asset volatility signals, or moving average information. The contemporaneous NIFTY50 return in the multivariate configuration provides an immediate systematic market-wide volatility signal. When a macro shock arrives, the market index signals elevated systematic risk while GARCH must wait one lag to observe the squared return impact — an information advantage particularly valuable during regime transitions when accurate early identification matters most for risk management. Third, freedom from distributional assumptions. GARCH’s Gaussian innovation assumption underestimates the heavy-tailed return distribution of Indian equities during stress events, as Fig. 1 illustrates: GARCH’s smooth conditional variance forecast visibly underestimates actual volatility spikes during the 2023–2024 test period. Machine learning models minimize mean squared prediction error without distributional assumptions, remaining calibrated to actual realized variance regardless of return distribution shape.

## **5.2  The Categorical Failure of Attention Mechanisms at Daily Horizons**

The finding that Transformer and AttentionForecaster architectures perform worse than GARCH(1,1) on both RMSE and directional accuracy is the most counterintuitive result of this study, given these architectures’ dominant performance in natural language processing and other sequential modelling domains. The primary explanation is architectural mismatch with the statistical properties of daily equity volatility. Attention mechanisms derive their power from learning long-range dependencies across sequences of hundreds to thousands of positions. In daily equity volatility data with a 20-step lookback window, there are no comparable long-range dependencies: the autocorrelation of squared returns decays substantially within 5–10 lags, and virtually all predictable variance is concentrated in very recent history that LSTM’s recurrence and XGBoost’s explicit lag features capture efficiently. The attention mechanism’s theoretical advantage simply does not engage at this horizon.  
The second explanation is noise amplification in attention weight computation. In a near-white-noise series like daily equity volatility residuals, attention scores computed via softmax(QKᵀ/√dₖ) assign disproportionate weight to high-magnitude historical observations simply because of their magnitude — not because they carry predictive information about the future. LSTM’s forget gate provides implicit regularization against this failure mode: it actively suppresses historical states whose information has become stale, preventing historical noise spikes from contaminating the current hidden state. XGBoost avoids the problem through gradient boosting’s implicit feature selection: uninformative lag features receive zero splitting importance and are effectively pruned. This failure at daily horizons does not preclude attention mechanisms from adding value at longer forecast horizons (weekly, monthly) or in multi-asset global forecasting where cross-instrument attention may discover genuine volatility contagion patterns.

## **5.3  Practical Implications for Risk Management**

The 2.3 percentage-point directional accuracy advantage of XGBoost-Sequential over GARCH(1,1) translates into approximately 11 additional correct volatility regime identifications over the 490-day test period per instrument. While this appears modest in absolute terms, the economic value depends critically on the asymmetric consequences of errors. A system that correctly identifies an impending high-volatility period — allowing a risk manager to reduce position sizes, increase hedges, or raise VaR limits proactively — can prevent losses that would otherwise materialise. HDFCBANK’s 61.0% directional accuracy is particularly relevant for practitioners managing concentrated banking sector positions, given HDFC Bank’s approximately 12% weight in NIFTY50 and the availability of liquid HDFCBANK and Bank NIFTY options for dynamic hedging on the NSE.  
The finding that univariate configurations are sufficient for all architectures has an additional practical benefit: simpler production pipelines. A univariate XGBoost-Sequential system requires only lagged squared returns and lagged volume as inputs, with no dependency on moving average calculations or cross-instrument market return data. This simplicity reduces operational risk — the risk that feature computation errors or data availability failures degrade production forecasts — and makes the system easier to validate, monitor, and explain to risk committees and regulators.

## **5.4  Architecture Selection Framework**

Synthesizing the empirical evidence, a clear architecture selection framework emerges. For one-step-ahead daily volatility forecasting in Indian equity markets, XGBoost-Sequential is the recommended primary architecture given its comprehensive outperformance of GARCH(1,1) on both RMSE (46% improvement) and directional accuracy (2.3pp improvement), its cross-instrument robustness, and its computational efficiency relative to neural architectures. The hybrid ARIMA-BiLSTM-XGBoost ensemble is recommended for production risk management environments where worst-case robustness is the primary concern, accepting a 16.3% RMSE penalty for 19.5% lower RMSE variance and 17.6% lower worst-case RMSE. BiLSTM is the recommended architecture for applications where price-level accuracy (rather than volatility) is the primary objective, where it has been shown to substantially outperform tree-based methods. Attention-based architectures (Transformer, AttentionForecaster) should not be deployed for daily one-step-ahead volatility forecasting at this horizon without compelling evidence of their advantage.

## **5.5  Limitations**

Several limitations warrant explicit acknowledgement. This study evaluates exclusively one-step-ahead (next-day) volatility forecasting using squared returns as the volatility proxy; realized variance estimated from intraday returns would provide a cleaner, lower-noise target and might yield different relative model rankings. GARCH(1,1) is the classical baseline without evaluating asymmetric variants (GJR-GARCH, EGARCH) that explicitly model the leverage effect; their inclusion would allow clearer attribution of the machine learning advantage to specific GARCH limitations. The four instruments are all large-cap liquid securities on the NSE; generalization to mid-cap instruments with different volatility dynamics remains to be established. Transaction costs and market impact are not modelled, so directional accuracy advantages may not fully translate to equivalent net trading returns after realistic cost accounting.

# **6  Conclusion**

This chapter has presented a systematic comparative evaluation of GARCH(1,1) against five advanced deep-learning architectures for Indian equity volatility forecasting, using a consistent dataset (January 2015 to December 2024), identical 80/20 chronological train-test split, and identical test period (2023–2024) for all 36 evaluated configurations. The fully consistent experimental design ensures that all performance comparisons are attributable to genuine architectural differences rather than dataset or evaluation window discrepancies. Six conclusions emerge with clear implications for researchers and practitioners.  
First, XGBoost-Sequential comprehensively outperforms GARCH(1,1) across all four instruments. Its 46% RMSE improvement and 2.3 percentage-point directional accuracy advantage reflect three structural advantages over the econometric benchmark: nonparametric threshold effect capturing through tree-based splits, access to richer feature information including market-wide volatility signals, and freedom from the Gaussian distributional assumption that causes GARCH to underestimate tail events. As Fig. 1 illustrates, GARCH’s smooth conditional variance forecast systematically lags actual volatility transitions while XGBoost’s regime partitioning provides sharper directional signals.  
Second, advanced neural architectures do not uniformly improve upon GARCH. BiLSTM provides a meaningful RMSE improvement (33.1%) but trails GARCH on directional accuracy (-2.8pp), making it less suitable for regime classification applications. Transformer and AttentionForecaster fail categorically, performing worse than GARCH in RMSE by 27% and 19% respectively, with directional accuracy well below the random baseline. The mechanistic explanation — architectural mismatch with the short-lag autocorrelation structure of daily equity volatility, combined with noise amplification in softmax attention weighting — provides clear conditions under which attention mechanisms are unsuitable: single-step daily forecasting with short lookback windows.  
Third, the hybrid ARIMA-BiLSTM-XGBoost ensemble provides a quantifiable robustness guarantee at 16.3% RMSE cost: 19.5% lower RMSE variance and 17.6% lower worst-case RMSE. For production risk management systems where catastrophic underestimation of volatility spikes carries asymmetrically large consequences, this tradeoff is demonstrably favorable.  
Fourth, multivariate feature augmentation provides negligible improvement (0.3% RMSE change) over univariate approaches for daily volatility forecasting across all architectures. Architecture selection dominates feature engineering as the primary performance driver, and univariate configurations provide equivalent accuracy with simpler production pipelines.  
Fifth, instrument-level heterogeneity in the magnitude of the machine learning advantage — RMSE gains ranging from 36.1% to 52.5% across instruments — motivates instrument-specific validation of production forecasting systems. The advantage is largest for HDFCBANK (52.5%) and smallest for TCS (36.1%), reflecting differences in each instrument’s leverage effect strength and autoregressive volatility structure.  
Sixth, the consistent architecture selection principle across all findings is clear and actionable: deploy XGBoost-Sequential as the primary component for daily volatility forecasting in Indian equity markets; use the hybrid ensemble for production environments requiring worst-case robustness guarantees; avoid attention-based architectures at this horizon; and use univariate feature configurations for simplicity without accuracy cost. These recommendations are grounded in the most consistent empirical evaluation of Indian equity volatility forecasting architectures conducted to date, using identical experimental conditions for all models being compared.

**Disclosure of Interests.** The authors have no competing interests to declare that are relevant to the content of this article.
